result:
### 5 opinionated best practices for Edge Delivery Services blocks

#### 1. Treat blocks as tiny, single-purpose components  
Focus: _blocks/components, maintainability_

- One responsibility per block: hero, teaser list, pricing table, etc.—not

This keeps JS/CSS small and testable.  
- Prefer **Block Collection** or existing blocks as a starting point before writing your own; only go custom when you truly need to.  
- Never simulate nesting with monstrous blocks; model repeated patterns as separate blocks and sections instead. Edge Delivery explicitly discourages nested blocks for a reason.

#### 2. Design authoring first, code second  
Focus: _authoring, content modeling_

- Start from the author's experience in Universal Editor or document-based authoring: _"Can a non-dev set this up in under a minute?"_ If not, simplify the model.  
- Use a small number of fields with clear labels and opinionated defaults; avoid 20+ toggles and magic flags.  
- For AEM-based authoring, keep block models (`component-models.json`) flat and predictable; share models across similar blocks instead of inventing a new schema for every variant.  
- Always prototype the block structure in a real page before finalizing the JS/CSS.

#### 3. Optimize for LHS 100, not JavaScript cleverness  
Focus: _performance_

- Assume every extra kilobyte of JS is guilty until proven innocent. Prefer semantic HTML + CSS over client-side logic wherever possible.  
- Respect the `scripts.js` phases: `loadEager` only what's needed for LCP, `loadLazy` for above-the-fold content, `loadDelayed` for analytics and non-critical fluff.  
- Keep the first section light: ≤3 blocks and one clear LCP element. If you cram carousels, accordions, and three promos into section 1, you're sabotaging Core Web Vitals.  
- Never inline heavy third-party widgets directly in blocks; use facades + lazy load or Intersection Observer.

#### 4. Make blocks boring to maintain  
Focus: _maintainability_

- Stick to the standard project layout: **one folder per block** with `block.css` and `block.js`. No "shared.css" monsters or mega-utils that every block imports.  
- Keep DOM transformations in `decorate()` small and local to the block; do not reach up into unrelated sections or global header/footer from block code.  
- Add at least minimal tests around complex interactions and data fetching.  
- Document each block in a "kitchen sink" page that authors and devs can see; treat that page as contract + visual regression baseline.

#### 5. Model content for reuse, not for pages  
Focus: _content modeling, authoring_

- Separate **content** (copy, media, structured data) from **layout** (sections, styles, ordering). Blocks should render structured fields; they shouldn't hard-wire specific page layouts.  
- Prefer reusable fragments for anything that appears in more than one place—hero promos, trust badges, footers, etc.  
- Use key–value / config-style fields sparingly and only for clearly technical options. Everything else should feel like normal content to authors.  
- Design models so they survive channel and authoring changes (doc-based ↔ AEM authoring ↔ future channels).

---

**References:**
- [Cloud 5 - Development best practices for EDS](https://experienceleague.adobe.com/en/docs/experience-manager-learn/cloud-service/expert-resources/cloud-5/season-4/cloud5-edge-delivery-services-best-practices)
- [EDS Overview](https://experienceleague.adobe.com/en/docs/experience-manager-cloud-service/content/edge-delivery/overview)
- [EDS FAQ](https://experienceleague.adobe.com/en/docs/experience-manager-cloud-service/content/edge-delivery/resources/faq)
- [Authoring with AEM for EDS](https://experienceleague.adobe.com/en/docs/experience-manager-cloud-service/content/edge-delivery/wysiwyg-authoring/authoring)
